### Ethnicities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Ethnicities")]

According to figures submitted by the [DANE](/wiki/National_Administrative_Department_of_Statistics "National Administrative Department of Statistics") census 2005,[[84]](#cite_note-84) the [ethnographic](/wiki/Ethnographic "Ethnographic") makeup of the city is:

* [White](/wiki/White_people "White people")/Mestizo: 93.4%
* [Black, mulatto, Afro-Colombian or Afro-descendant](/wiki/Afro-Colombian "Afro-Colombian"): 6.5%
* [Indigenous Amerindians](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas"): 0.1%

During the 17th and 18th centuries, Medellín received many immigrants from Spain.[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86) Most Indigenous peoples died from the introduction of Asiatic diseases, and many of those who survived intermarried with early Spanish settlers, who were mostly men; later, Spanish women also began to immigrate. Thousands of Irish, Scottish and English who settled in Antioquia fought for the Colombian army during independence.[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88) During the 19th and 20th centuries, immigrants arrived from Italy, Germany, United Kingdom, France, Portugal, Lebanon, Palestine and Syria.[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92) Many people from Medellín are referred to as [Paisas](/wiki/Paisa_Region "Paisa Region"), people of mainly Spanish ancestry, a lot of them Basque. There is a small Afro-Colombian and Zambo-Colombian (people of Indigenous and African descent) population.

[![Total fertility rate by municipality Valle de Aburrá (Medellín metropolitan area), 2024](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Isfaburra24.png/250px-Isfaburra24.png)](/wiki/File:Isfaburra24.png)

Total fertility rate by municipality Valle de Aburrá, 2024

The [Chocó Department](/wiki/Choc%C3%B3_Department "Chocó Department") is just west of Antioquia, and is home to many Afro-Colombian and Zambo-Colombian migrants to Medellín and its vicinity. Migration from the Colombian Caribbean coast has been important, especially that of young people who come to study in Medellín universities and remain to work in the city. The main foreign immigration is of [Ecuadorians](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador") and [Venezuelans](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela") in informal trade.

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/02-063_Palacio_de_la_Cultura_Rafael_Uribe_Uribe.JPG/250px-02-063_Palacio_de_la_Cultura_Rafael_Uribe_Uribe.JPG)](/wiki/File:02-063_Palacio_de_la_Cultura_Rafael_Uribe_Uribe.JPG)

[Rafael Uribe Uribe Palace of Culture](/wiki/Rafael_Uribe_Uribe_Palace_of_Culture "Rafael Uribe Uribe Palace of Culture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/La_Raza_de_Rodrigo_Arenas_Betancur-Medellin.JPG/250px-La_Raza_de_Rodrigo_Arenas_Betancur-Medellin.JPG)](/wiki/File:La_Raza_de_Rodrigo_Arenas_Betancur-Medellin.JPG)

*[Monumento a la Raza](/wiki/Monumento_a_la_Raza_(Medell%C3%ADn) "Monumento a la Raza (Medellín)")* by [Rodrigo Arenas Betancur](/wiki/Rodrigo_Arenas "Rodrigo Arenas"), located in [La Alpujarra Administrative Center](/wiki/La_Alpujarra_Administrative_Center "La Alpujarra Administrative Center")

The culture in Medellín is strongly linked to a broader Paisa culture (see next section) whose unique attributes include their Spanish accent, cuisine, and hospitality. Today, Medellín has several cultural attractions for the public including approximately 40 museums, 21 public parks, 28 theaters, and several public libraries.[[93]](#cite_note-93) The city also contains several fixtures that have been designated [National monuments of Colombia](/wiki/National_monuments_of_Colombia "National monuments of Colombia"). The city is recognized as a "[Design City](/wiki/Design_Cities_(UNESCO) "Design Cities (UNESCO)")" by UNESCO.

Most people in Medellín are [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), as reflected by Medellín's several churches and religious activities. Among the most representative churches are the [Metropolitan Cathedral](/wiki/Metropolitan_Cathedral_of_Medell%C3%ADn "Metropolitan Cathedral of Medellín"), the largest cathedral in the world built entirely in baked brick. There is also the [Basilica of Our Lady of Candelaria](/wiki/Basilica_of_Our_Lady_of_Candelaria "Basilica of Our Lady of Candelaria"), which was the official cathedral until 1931; the [Church of San Ignacio](/w/index.php?title=Church_of_San_Ignacio_(Medell%C3%ADn)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Church of San Ignacio (Medellín) (page does not exist)"), Baroque on the outside and Colonial on inside; the [Church of San José](/w/index.php?title=Church_of_San_Jos%C3%A9_(Medell%C3%ADn)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Church of San José (Medellín) (page does not exist)"); the [Church of San Antonio](/w/index.php?title=Church_of_San_Antonio_(Medell%C3%ADn)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Church of San Antonio (Medellín) (page does not exist)"), which has one of the biggest domes in Colombia; the [Church of San José del Poblado](/w/index.php?title=Church_of_San_Jos%C3%A9_del_Poblado&action=edit&redlink=1 "Church of San José del Poblado (page does not exist)"), located in the Parque del Poblado where the first European settlement in the [Aburrá Valley](/wiki/Aburr%C3%A1_Valley "Aburrá Valley") was founded in 1616; and the [Church of Nuestra Señora de Belén](/w/index.php?title=Church_of_Nuestra_Se%C3%B1ora_de_Bel%C3%A9n_(Medell%C3%ADn)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Church of Nuestra Señora de Belén (Medellín) (page does not exist)"). All of these churches have religious art and are located in the center of the city, which facilitates journeys among them.

In December, the city is covered with thousands of fairy lights, creating the famous *[Alumbrados](/wiki/Christmas_lights_in_Medell%C3%ADn "Christmas lights in Medellín")* (Christmas lights), which are considered by the [National Geographic](/wiki/National_Geographic_Society "National Geographic Society") as one of the ten most beautiful in the world,[[94]](#cite_note-94) and which can be seen mainly on *La Playa* Avenue and the Medellín River.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Pano_Plazo_Botero.jpg/500px-Pano_Plazo_Botero.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pano_Plazo_Botero.jpg)

Plaza Botero, in the background the [Museum of Antioquia](/wiki/Museum_of_Antioquia "Museum of Antioquia")

[Plaza Mayor](/wiki/Plaza_Mayor,_Medell%C3%ADn "Plaza Mayor, Medellín") is the epicenter of large events and business. By a decree of the municipal government, between 1980 and 1990 all developments or tall buildings necessarily included a sculpture of a famous artist. That is one reason why Medellín has the largest number of sculptures per square kilometer in Colombia.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Furthermore, the city has several festivals and exhibitions year-round. The [silletero](/wiki/Sillero "Sillero") tradition is also closely tied to the region and is considered a part of Colombian [cultural heritage](/wiki/Cultural_heritage "Cultural heritage"). Since 1957, this tradition has continued in the Silleta Parade that takes place during the annual [Festival of the Flowers](/wiki/Festival_of_the_Flowers "Festival of the Flowers"), where silletas designed with flower arrangements are carried.

Works of many prominent artists, both local and foreign, can be seen on the streets of the city. Some artists who stand out are the masters [Rodrigo Arenas Betancur](/wiki/Rodrigo_Arenas_Betancur "Rodrigo Arenas Betancur") and [Fernando Botero](/wiki/Fernando_Botero "Fernando Botero").

### Paisa culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Paisa culture")]

Main article: [Paisa region](/wiki/Paisa_region "Paisa region")

The inhabitants of Medellín are often called *Antioqueños* (people of Antioquia) after their province, rather than *Medellínenses* (people of Medellín) after their city. They are also often known as *[Paisas](/wiki/Paisa_Region "Paisa Region")*, a name which some suggest comes from the coffee growers. The term *Paisa* comes from the word *paisano*, meaning "fellow countryman". *Paisas* make up one of the five different regional cultures within Colombia. In addition to Antioquia, the [Paisa region](/wiki/Paisa_region "Paisa region") includes the departments of [Caldas](/wiki/Caldas_Department "Caldas Department"), [Risaralda](/wiki/Risaralda_Department "Risaralda Department"), [Quindio](/wiki/Quindio "Quindio") and some towns of [Valle del Cauca](/wiki/Valle_del_Cauca "Valle del Cauca") and [Tolima](/wiki/Department_of_Tolima "Department of Tolima"). Although Paisa culture is dominant in Medellín, the so-called "Paisa Capital", the city is becoming more cosmopolitan, now offering music from other regions of Colombia ([Vallenato](/wiki/Vallenato "Vallenato") and [Chocó](/wiki/Department_of_Choc%C3%B3 "Department of Chocó")), and a variety of restaurants including Chinese, Cuban, and Argentinian.

The Paisa culture has a Spanish background, and is traditionally Catholic, and entrepreneurial.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Paisas are said to speak softly and slowly, to smile easily, and to love music, poetry, soccer, bargaining in the markets, and parties. They are proud of their city, and work hard to keep it clean, stemming from the campaign begun in the 1980s, "Depende tambien de ti, darle amor a Medellín" (It depends on you too to give love to Medellín). The Medellín weekend nightlife, in discos, pubs, parks, and certain dedicated streets, is traditionally called *rumba*.[[95]](#cite_note-MattBrown-95)

### Festivals and events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Festivals and events")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Alumbrado_2011_-_AV_La_Playa-2.jpg/250px-Alumbrado_2011_-_AV_La_Playa-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alumbrado_2011_-_AV_La_Playa-2.jpg)

[Christmas Lighting](/wiki/Christmas_lights_in_Medell%C3%ADn "Christmas lights in Medellín") on *La Playa* Avenue in 2011

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Desfile_de_Silleteros2007-%282%29Medellin.JPG/250px-Desfile_de_Silleteros2007-%282%29Medellin.JPG)](/wiki/File:Desfile_de_Silleteros2007-(2)Medellin.JPG)

An example of a *silleta* at the [Festival of the Flowers](/wiki/Festival_of_the_Flowers "Festival of the Flowers")

* **[Festival of the Flowers](/wiki/Festival_of_the_Flowers "Festival of the Flowers")** is held late July to early August. The Festival, plus many other activities and festivities, is the main axis the [Desfile de Silleteros](/w/index.php?title=Desfile_de_Silleteros&action=edit&redlink=1 "Desfile de Silleteros (page does not exist)"), a colorful and artistic parade of flowers filled saddles on the back for their own grower and already well known internationally.
* **[Christmas lighting in Medellín](/wiki/Christmas_lights_in_Medell%C3%ADn "Christmas lights in Medellín")** is a traditional seasonal event where the city hangs millions of Christmas lights and holds light shows and other cultural events. National Geographic ranked Medellín Christmas lights display as one of the top 10 places to see Christmas lights in the world.
* **[International Poetry Festival](/wiki/International_Poetry_Festival_of_Medell%C3%ADn "International Poetry Festival of Medellín")** is an annual congregation of poets, who give at public their poems and readings of a peculiar way mass in parks, auditoriums, neighborhoods and towns near Medellín. This event has been awarded the [Right Livelihood Award](/wiki/Right_Livelihood_Award "Right Livelihood Award"), the prelude to the Nobel Peace Prize by the Right Livelihood Foundation of Sweden, and its aim is to strive for peace through poetry.[[96]](#cite_note-96) The XVII International Poetry Festival of Medellín was performed between 14 and 22 July 2007, with the participation of over 80 poets from around 55 nations. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* **[International Tango Festival](/w/index.php?title=International_Tango_Festival_(Medell%C3%ADn)&action=edit&redlink=1 "International Tango Festival (Medellín) (page does not exist)")** is an expression of the tango culture that Medellín adopted as their own. This is a legacy of the bard [Carlos Gardel](/wiki/Carlos_Gardel "Carlos Gardel"), who died in this city in a plane crash in 1935. Among the activities of the Festival highlights the Tangovía, where people took to the streets to dance, listen to [tango](/wiki/Tango_music "Tango music"), [milonga](/wiki/Milonga_(dance) "Milonga (dance)") and obviously having fun at the behest of culture.
* **Book and Culture Festival** is one of the most important cultural events in Medellín, named itself Festival, rather than Fair, due to its magical atmosphere that revolves around art, books and culture.[[97]](#cite_note-97) The Festival is hosted for 10 days at the Botanical Garden in the month of September, and it is held with the participation of both national and international participants and exhibitors from all genres. The Book and Culture Festival is free entry, and aims to promote reading through different workshops. This event is sponsored by the Mayor of Medellín.
* **[Feria internacional del caballo](/w/index.php?title=Feria_internacional_del_caballo_(Medell%C3%ADn)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Feria internacional del caballo (Medellín) (page does not exist)")** Since 2009 was added to the horse paisa culture the Feria internacional del caballo, held in October, an exhibition with participants from several countries and involving varied horse races.[[98]](#cite_note-98)
* **[Bullfighting Festival of La Macarena](/w/index.php?title=Bullfighting_Festival_of_La_Macarena&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bullfighting Festival of La Macarena (page does not exist)")** held every year between January and February.

### Museums and other venues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Museums and other venues")]

Medellín has about 40 galleries, which are one of the main attractions of the city. Some of the main museums include the following:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/El_Castillo-exterior.JPG/250px-El_Castillo-exterior.JPG)](/wiki/File:El_Castillo-exterior.JPG)

Museum El Castillo

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Tumba_de_Santa_Laura_Montoya_Upegui.jpg/250px-Tumba_de_Santa_Laura_Montoya_Upegui.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tumba_de_Santa_Laura_Montoya_Upegui.jpg)

Tomb of Saint [Laura Montoya Upegui](/wiki/Laura_of_Saint_Catherine_of_Siena "Laura of Saint Catherine of Siena")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Museo_Cementerio_San_Pedro%281%29-Medellin.JPG/250px-Museo_Cementerio_San_Pedro%281%29-Medellin.JPG)](/wiki/File:Museo_Cementerio_San_Pedro(1)-Medellin.JPG)

[San Pedro Cemetery Museum](/wiki/San_Pedro_Cemetery_Museum "San Pedro Cemetery Museum")

* **[Museum of Antioquia](/wiki/Museum_of_Antioquia "Museum of Antioquia")** was the second museum founded in Colombia and the first in the department of [Antioquia](/wiki/Antioquia_Department "Antioquia Department"). It is located at the heart of the old city hall next to [Botero Plaza](/wiki/Botero_Plaza "Botero Plaza"), and houses 187 works of [Fernando Botero](/wiki/Fernando_Botero "Fernando Botero").
* **[Museum of Modern Art of Medellín](/wiki/Museum_of_Modern_Art_of_Medell%C3%ADn "Museum of Modern Art of Medellín")** (MAMM), founded in 1978, has a valuable collection of contemporary art comprising sculptures, assemblages, paintings, photographs, and prints by local and foreign artists. One of its major attractions is the collection of almost all the works of the Antioquian painter [Débora Arango](/wiki/D%C3%A9bora_Arango "Débora Arango"). Another of its main attractions is the projection of cinema-art. It is also headquarters of the International Biennale of Video in September.
* **[University Museum, University of Antioquia](/w/index.php?title=University_Museum,_University_of_Antioquia&action=edit&redlink=1 "University Museum, University of Antioquia (page does not exist)")**, was created in 1942, is located in University City. The area of [anthropology](/wiki/Anthropology "Anthropology") exhibits a collection of 18 000 pieces of pre-Columbian [pottery](/wiki/Pottery "Pottery"), [stone](/wiki/Stone_artefact "Stone artefact"), [shell](/wiki/Mollusc_shell "Mollusc shell"), metal and textiles, the second richest in the country, and a complete ethnographic collection. The area of visual arts includes contemporary painting and sculpture, and includes 1200 pieces of contemporary artists. The area of History at the university collects in a thousand pieces and documents the 200 years of existence of the university, and the natural sciences section displays 5400 pieces, including native and exotic stuffed animals, skins for study, minerals and fossils.
* **[Museum El Castillo](/wiki/Museum_El_Castillo "Museum El Castillo")**, built in 1930 in medieval Gothic and open to the public in 1971, has French-style gardens, explosions room, library and concert hall for 250 people. It has permanent exhibits of porcelain and glass, stained glass, music, sculpture, piano and ballet.
* **[Interactive Museum EPM](/wiki/Interactive_Museum_EPM "Interactive Museum EPM")** environment is part of [Barefoot Park](/wiki/Barefoot_Park "Barefoot Park"). Receives 1000 visit a day, mostly students. This is an educational tour of 22 rooms spread over four buildings in which, with technological resources and entertaining way, is explained and interacts with the physical principles of water, energy, gas and telecommunications. It is funded and managed by [Empresas Públicas de Medellín](/wiki/Empresas_P%C3%BAblicas_de_Medell%C3%ADn "Empresas Públicas de Medellín").
* **[Mineralogy Museum](/w/index.php?title=Mineralogy_Museum&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mineralogy Museum (page does not exist)")** is located in the School of Mines of the [National University of Colombia](/wiki/National_University_of_Colombia "National University of Colombia"). It has a permanent exhibition of a total of 2778 specimens; its collections are recognized by mineralogists and experts among the best museums in South America.
* **[San Pedro Cemetery Museum](/wiki/San_Pedro_Cemetery_Museum "San Pedro Cemetery Museum")** was built in 1842, became a museum in 1998 and declared a [national monument](/wiki/National_monument "National monument") in 1999. This place is an integral part of cultural and architectural heritage of Medellín. Although it falls under the category of sculpture and architecture works representing the funerary art, the space has begun to emerge as a new venue for artistic dissemination. It has preserved collections of local and national art and, on full moon nights, there are concerts, shows, storytelling, theater and dance. Undoubtedly the most significant are the funerary monuments in memory of prominent figures in national history.
* **[House Museum Master Pedro Nel Gómez](/w/index.php?title=House_Museum_Master_Pedro_Nel_G%C3%B3mez&action=edit&redlink=1 "House Museum Master Pedro Nel Gómez (page does not exist)")** was the home of the master [Pedro Nel Gómez](/wiki/Pedro_Nel_G%C3%B3mez "Pedro Nel Gómez"); it became a museum in 1975 with the donation by the artist and his family of the works in it. It has 1500 works, 200 square meters (2,200 [square feet](/wiki/Square_foot "Square foot")) of fresco painting and an art library with over 500 volumes.
* **[Casa Museo Gardeliana](/w/index.php?title=Casa_Museo_Gardeliana&action=edit&redlink=1 "Casa Museo Gardeliana (page does not exist)")** disseminates the culture and history of the [tango](/wiki/Tango "Tango"). It was declared a Cultural Heritage and Historic Monument by the Council of Medellín in 2002. It was founded on 14 February 1973, by the Argentine Leonardo Nieto Jarbon in the neighborhood Manrique, in a simple, traditional house. The tango singer [Carlos Gardel](/wiki/Carlos_Gardel "Carlos Gardel") met his death in Medellín in accident airplane crash. There has long existed in the city a deep tango culture. Some plaques testify the visit to the house of personalities from politics, entertainment and literature, as the Argentine writer Jorge Luis Borges.
* **[Parque Explora](/wiki/Parque_Explora "Parque Explora")** is an interactive museum focused on science, technology and many other aspects of knowledge and creativity. It provides, among other attractions, the largest freshwater aquarium in South America that houses fish of the Amazon and Orinoco rivers.
* **[Planetarium of Medellín](/wiki/Planetarium_of_Medell%C3%ADn "Planetarium of Medellín")** is located in front of the [Parque de Los Deseos](/w/index.php?title=Parque_de_Los_Deseos&action=edit&redlink=1 "Parque de Los Deseos (page does not exist)"). The space includes telescopes and a projection room for 300 people, a dome 17.5 meters (57 feet) in diameter, and permanent exhibitions for all ages on Earth and space. Its services include an auditorium, library and a permanent exhibition on space history.

Other prominent museums are Centro Cultural Banco de la República, [Entomological Museum Francisco Luis Gallego](/w/index.php?title=Entomological_Museum_Francisco_Luis_Gallego&action=edit&redlink=1 "Entomological Museum Francisco Luis Gallego (page does not exist)"), Casa Museo Santa Fe, Museum of Natural Sciences, Ethnographic Museum Miguel Ángel Builes and the [Mother Laura Ethnographic Museum](/wiki/Laura_of_Saint_Catherine_of_Siena "Laura of Saint Catherine of Siena").[[99]](#cite_note-99)

### Public art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Public art")]

Medellín is well known for having great public art, and there are many buildings that have prominent sculptures outside them. In addition to the sculpture park of Fernando Botero, there are many other sculptural gems by a variety of contemporary and modernist artists scattered around the city. They include works by the artist Hugo Zapata amongst others.

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Bandeja_paisa_y_tazon_de_frejoles_del_Chocolos_30062011.jpg/250px-Bandeja_paisa_y_tazon_de_frejoles_del_Chocolos_30062011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bandeja_paisa_y_tazon_de_frejoles_del_Chocolos_30062011.jpg)

[Bandeja paisa](/wiki/Bandeja_paisa "Bandeja paisa") is the representative food of Medellín and [Antioquia](/wiki/Antioquia_Department "Antioquia Department").

The cuisine of Medellín is again tied to the larger culture of Antioquia. A typical regional dish is the [bandeja paisa](/wiki/Bandeja_paisa "Bandeja paisa"), meaning the "paisa platter", which usually includes beans, rice, pork cracklings (or "chicharrón"), [chorizo](/wiki/Chorizo "Chorizo"), a fried egg, [patacónes](/wiki/Tostones "Tostones"), salad and avocado. It is traditionally served on a tray slab or wood. It is traditional to consume meals with arepas, which are made with cooked, milled, and mixed corn without further ingredients.

For breakfast, it is common to accompany the food with [hot chocolate](/wiki/Hot_chocolate "Hot chocolate") and possibly a slice of cheese to dunk in the drink. [Parva](/wiki/Parva_(food) "Parva (food)") is a traditional component of the Antioquian gastronomy as well. This name is given to a wide variety of sweet and savory baked goods, including [pan de queso](/wiki/Pan_de_queso "Pan de queso"), [pan de bono](/wiki/Pan_de_bono "Pan de bono"), [buñuelos](/wiki/Bu%C3%B1uelo "Buñuelo"), and pastries.

Other foods that are typical in the region stem from Colombian food industries, such as [Postobón](/wiki/Postob%C3%B3n "Postobón")'s Colombian soft drinks, the regional [aguardiente](/wiki/Aguardiente#Colombia "Aguardiente") known as *Aguardiente Antioqueño*, and Jet chocolate bars.

### Creative Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Creative Tourism")]

Medellín has developed its [Cultural Tourism](/wiki/Cultural_tourism#Creative_Tourism "Cultural tourism") through various activities such as cooking workshops, dancing classes or arts and craft. Medellín is an official Creative Tourism City[[100]](#cite_note-100) thanks to its cultural richness and its wide array of activities between tradition and innovation.

## Sports and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Sports and recreation")]

Soccer is the most popular sport and is widely played in the city. Medellín has two professional clubs: [Independiente Medellín](/wiki/Independiente_Medell%C3%ADn "Independiente Medellín"), founded in 1913, and [Atlético Nacional](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Nacional "Atlético Nacional"), founded in 1935. Both teams play at the Atanasio Girardot Stadium. The city has a soccer legacy thanks to notable players such as [René Higuita](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Higuita "René Higuita"), [Iván Córdoba](/wiki/Iv%C3%A1n_C%C3%B3rdoba "Iván Córdoba"), [Andrés Escobar](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9s_Escobar "Andrés Escobar"), [Víctor Aristizábal](/wiki/V%C3%ADctor_Aristiz%C3%A1bal "Víctor Aristizábal"), [Jackson Martínez](/wiki/Jackson_Mart%C3%ADnez "Jackson Martínez") and many others. Other notable sportsmen of the city are the professional golfer and PGA Tour player [Camilo Villegas](/wiki/Camilo_Villegas "Camilo Villegas"), Medellín-born Israeli Olympic [show jumping](/wiki/Show_jumping "Show jumping") rider [Daniel Bluman](/wiki/Daniel_Bluman "Daniel Bluman"), and the retired [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One"), [Champ Car](/wiki/Champ_Car "Champ Car"), and [IndyCar](/wiki/IndyCar "IndyCar") driver [Roberto Guerrero](/wiki/Roberto_Guerrero "Roberto Guerrero").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Atanacio_girardot_antes_de_la_apertura_de_los_juegos.jpg/250px-Atanacio_girardot_antes_de_la_apertura_de_los_juegos.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atanacio_girardot_antes_de_la_apertura_de_los_juegos.jpg)

[Atanasio Girardot Sports Complex](/wiki/Atanasio_Girardot_Sports_Complex "Atanasio Girardot Sports Complex")

A popular sport in Medellín, and generally throughout Antioquia, is [horseback riding](/wiki/Equitation "Equitation"); therefore, it has encouraged trade and production of tools and equipments for this activity, such as chairs and horseshoes for export. During the Festival of the Flowers, the [cavalcades](/wiki/Cavalcade "Cavalcade") achieved a [Guinness World Record](/wiki/Guinness_World_Record "Guinness World Record") in 1996 and 1999.

The city also has two Colombian professional basketball teams: the [Arrieros](/wiki/Arriero "Arriero") of Antioquia and the Mountain Academy.

### Hosting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Hosting")]

The [Atanasio Girardot Sports Complex](/wiki/Atanasio_Girardot_Sports_Complex "Atanasio Girardot Sports Complex") is the main sports arena complex of the city. It covers an area of 280,000 m2 (3,013,895 sq ft) and encompasses fields and stadiums for practice and competition for 34 sports. This is the headquarters of most professional sports leagues in Medellín. The sports complex has hosted several events, including the [2011 FIFA U-20 World Cup](/wiki/2011_FIFA_U-20_World_Cup "2011 FIFA U-20 World Cup"), the [2010 South American Games](/wiki/2010_South_American_Games "2010 South American Games"), and the [2001 Copa América](/wiki/2001_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2001 Copa América").

In 2011, Medellín hosted the 15th IAAF [Pan American Race Walking Cup](/wiki/Pan_American_Race_Walking_Cup "Pan American Race Walking Cup"). Athletes and teams from the countries in North, South, and Central American as well as the Caribbean Islands participated. In 2013, Medellín hosted the IAAF [Pan American Junior Athletics Championships](/wiki/Pan_American_Junior_Athletics_Championships "Pan American Junior Athletics Championships") for track and field. Again, athletes and teams from the countries in North, South, and Central America as well as the Caribbean Islands participated. The junior athletes competed as a test trial for the [2018 Summer Youth Olympics](/wiki/2018_Summer_Youth_Olympics "2018 Summer Youth Olympics").

[Medellín bid](/wiki/Medell%C3%ADn_bid_for_the_2018_Summer_Youth_Olympics "Medellín bid for the 2018 Summer Youth Olympics") to host the 2018 Summer Youth Olympics but was defeated by [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires") in the 4 July 2013 vote.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

### Cycling and BMX

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Cycling and BMX")]

Another representative sport in the city is [cycling](/wiki/Cycle_sport "Cycle sport"). Its respective sport venue is the Martin Rodriguez Velodrome, named after Colombian [road racing cyclist](/wiki/Road_racing_cyclist "Road racing cyclist") [Martín Emilio Rodríguez](/wiki/Mart%C3%ADn_Emilio_Rodr%C3%ADguez "Martín Emilio Rodríguez"). [BMX](/wiki/BMX "BMX") is also catered for, and its main venue is the BMX Track Antonio Roldán Betancur. The retired cycling three-time [Tour de France](/wiki/Tour_de_France "Tour de France") stage winner and World Time-Trial Champion [Santiago Botero](/wiki/Santiago_Botero "Santiago Botero") and the Olympic Gold Medalist and multiple BMX World Champion [Mariana Pajón](/wiki/Mariana_Paj%C3%B3n "Mariana Pajón") are both from Medellín.

[EnCicla](/wiki/EnCicla "EnCicla") is the name of Medellín's [bicycle sharing system](/wiki/Bicycle_sharing_system "Bicycle sharing system"), the first bike sharing program in Latin America and the only one of its kind in Colombia. It was the shared winner of the [Sustainable Transport Award](/wiki/Sustainable_Transport_Award "Sustainable Transport Award") in 2012 along with [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"). EnCicla is the result of an agreement between the metropolitan area of the Valley of Aburrá and [EAFIT University](/wiki/EAFIT_University "EAFIT University"). EnCicla is integrated within the city's existing infrastructure of cycle routes, mass transit, and public transport systems. It is available from Monday to Friday from 5:30 am to 8 pm from every EnCicla station.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Symbols")]

Main article: [Coat of arms of Medellín (Colombia)](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Medell%C3%ADn_(Colombia) "Coat of arms of Medellín (Colombia)")

The [Medellín coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Medell%C3%ADn_(Colombia) "Coat of arms of Medellín (Colombia)") is the oldest emblem of the city, having its origins in a grant by King [Charles II of Spain](/wiki/Charles_II_of_Spain "Charles II of Spain") by royal decree issued in Madrid on 31 March of 1678:

> ...On a blue field shield, a very thick and round tower, battlemented, an inescutcheon of fifteen parts – seven blue and eight gold, on each of the sides a smaller tower, and in between them an image of Our Lady on a cloud with her son in her arms...

| Heraldic version | Versions of the municipality bodies | |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  |  |  |
| With the shape called modern French style | Version used by the Mayor | Version used by the Council |

However, a more refined and structured in heraldic language, though not official, would be:

> In an [Azure](/wiki/Azure_(heraldry) "Azure (heraldry)") field, a round tower of gold stands, masoned and clarified by sable, with a checkered escutcheon of 15 pieces – 7 blue and 8 gold (coat of arms of [House of Portocarrero](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Portocarrero&action=edit&redlink=1 "House of Portocarrero (page does not exist)")) – stamped with an ancient crown of gold between its two towers, with a cloud upon which stands the image of Our Lady of Candelaria carrying the Child in her left arm, a candle in her right hand, radiant, she is flanked by forming clouds from each county.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Escudo_de_Medellin_Antiguo.svg/250px-Escudo_de_Medellin_Antiguo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Escudo_de_Medellin_Antiguo.svg)

First coat of arms of Medellín

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Escudo_de_armas_de_medellin-1678.jpg/250px-Escudo_de_armas_de_medellin-1678.jpg)](/wiki/File:Escudo_de_armas_de_medellin-1678.jpg)

Coat of Arms of Medellín in 1678

The blazon has remained over time since it was granted, without further changes to the aesthetics, it is noteworthy that there are different stylistic versions between the Mayor and City Council also meets aesthetic not heraldic standards.

### Flag

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Flag")]

To strengthen the links with the region, the municipality adopted the [flag of the Department of Antioquia](/wiki/Flag_of_the_Department_of_Antioquia "Flag of the Department of Antioquia"), which was added to the coat of arms of the city, so that these could make difference. The flag consists of two horizontal stripes of equal proportions, the white top and green bottom, and in the middle between two strips is located the coat. The white color symbolizes purity, integrity, obedience, firmness and eloquence. The green represents hope, abundance, freedom and faith.

### Anthem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Anthem")]

The anthem of the city of Medellín is "Anthem of Antioquia", according to Decree No. 151 20 February 2002, Article 10, which says: "Anthem of Medellín. To unify the ideals of the region the Antioquia anthem is adopted (lyrics by [Epifanio Mejia](/wiki/Epifanio_Mejia "Epifanio Mejia") and music by Gonzalo Vidal) as the Anthem of the Municipality of Medellín. It will be sung in all official functions where the Mayor of Medellín is present."
